topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. Subject line: "Update Regarding [Name]'s Celebration." My fingers hovered over the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the words. *Cancelled*. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I reread the message three times, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that suggested a joke. There was nothing. Just a flat, impersonal declaration. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small living room, the silence amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I kept glancing toward the front door, half-expecting someone to burst in, to explain the absurdity of it all. My hands started to sweat, and I pressed them against my temples, trying to slow the rapid rhythm of my breathing.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I whispered to the empty room, the sound barely audible over the hum of the refrigerator. The confirmation text was stark, brutally final. No explanation, just the abrupt cessation of plans I had meticulously organized for weeks. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain back just an inch, peering out at the street as if the answer might be written in the traffic. A strange, prickly sensation crawled up my neck. I felt a sudden, intense need to check my phone repeatedly, a compulsive habit taking over. Every notification seemed to carry a weight of implication, and I couldn't shake the feeling that something significant had just shifted, something decidedly unwelcome.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam. The cheerful invitations, the coordinated decorations I’d spent hours sourcing—all rendered meaningless by this single notification. A slow, heavy feeling settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked to the kitchen, gripped the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white. The air suddenly felt thick, too heavy to move through. I started tapping my foot against the linoleum floor, a nervous, uneven beat that mirrored the frantic scrambling happening inside my head. I needed a distraction, anything to break this suffocating stillness, but every thought circled back to the fact that the anticipated event simply did not exist anymore.
